locked_facts: Stress = Force/Area: σ = F/A (internal restoring force per unit area)
Strain = ΔL/L (fractional change in dimension, dimensionless)
Hooke's law: stress ∝ strain in elastic limit → σ = Yε
Bulk modulus B = -VΔP/ΔV (resistance to volume change)
Shear modulus G = shear stress/shear strain
Stress-strain curve regions: proportional limit → elastic limit → yield point → ultimate strength → fracture
truth_statements: Stress is not the applied force but the internal restoring force per unit area within the material
Young's modulus is a material property that measures stiffness — higher Y means the material is harder to stretch
Beyond the elastic limit the material undergoes permanent deformation and does not return to original shape
The area under the stress-strain curve up to any point represents the energy stored per unit volume
Steel has a higher Young's modulus than rubber, meaning steel is stiffer though rubber is more elastic (returns to shape over larger deformation)

aha_moment.what_to_show: Full stress-strain curve for a ductile material: linear region (Hooke's law), elastic limit (beyond this = permanent damage), yield point, necking, and fracture. Loading and unloading in the elastic region: returns to origin. Loading beyond elastic limit: permanent set visible.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Stress-strain curve with regions colour-coded: green (elastic), yellow (plastic), red (necking/fracture), with load-unload cycle shown
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think materials either stretch or break — seeing the rich behaviour between elastic and fracture (yielding, strain hardening, necking) reveals the full story

simulation_states.label: Stress and strain defined
simulation_states.description: A wire stretched by a force. Stress σ = F/A (force per unit area). Strain ε = ΔL/L (fractional extension). Both shown with numerical values.
simulation_states.emphasis: Stress is force per area. Strain is fractional change in length.

simulation_states.label: Hooke's law region
simulation_states.description: Force gradually increased. Stress-strain graph is linear. Y = slope. Material returns to original length when force removed. Spring analogy.
simulation_states.emphasis: In the elastic region: σ = Yε. Material bounces back like a spring.

simulation_states.label: Full stress-strain curve
simulation_states.description: Continue loading past elastic limit. Yield point: permanent deformation begins. Strain hardening. Ultimate tensile strength. Necking. Fracture. Each region labelled.
simulation_states.emphasis: Beyond elastic limit: permanent deformation. Material doesn't fully recover.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student pulls a virtual wire. Stress-strain curve builds in real time. Release force: see elastic recovery vs permanent set. Compare materials: steel, rubber, glass.
simulation_states.emphasis: Pull the wire — watch the stress-strain curve build. Release — does it return to origin?

common_misconceptions.belief: Rubber is more elastic than steel
common_misconceptions.correction: In physics, 'elastic' means returning to original shape after deformation. Steel has a HIGHER Young's modulus and returns to its original shape for small deformations more reliably. Rubber is more flexible (lower Y) but not more elastic in the physics sense.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show steel and rubber stress-strain curves — steel has higher Y (stiffer) but both return to origin within elastic limits

common_misconceptions.belief: Stress is the same as force
common_misconceptions.correction: Stress = Force/Area (σ = F/A). The same force produces different stress depending on the cross-sectional area. A thin wire has much higher stress than a thick rod under the same force.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Same force on thin vs thick wire — thin wire shows much higher stress and more elongation
